questions.text: I see high-stakes situations as opportunities to try bold new approaches.
I would sacrifice a personal passion if pursuing it took resources away from people who depend on me.
I hold the view that the moral significance of suffering is what makes ethical reasoning concrete rather than abstract.
I believe that the capacity to remain analytically engaged while others are emotionally overwhelmed is essential for navigating complex human problems.
I feel that reserving weekends for personal restoration is essential, even when others want my time.
I believe that the capacity for calm, evidence-based reasoning during human crises is what distinguishes effective response from well-meaning but disorganized effort.
I value directness in communication above all else.
I notice when people adjust their vocabulary depending on who they are speaking to.
I rarely consider whether the timing of a message carries meaning beyond its content.
I feel that passing along an opportunity to someone better suited, even when I want it, is the right call.
I am quick to feel a jolt of alarm in ambiguous situations.
I feel that my personal taste in food, travel, and entertainment should guide shared decisions.
I believe that my constitutional calm is not a mask — it is genuinely how I experience the world.
I feel that every personal indulgence should be weighed against what it could do for someone else.
I notice that my emotional recovery time is remarkably short — I bounce back without effort.
I keep my commitments organized in a single trusted system so nothing falls through the cracks.
I prefer making decisions slowly and carefully over making them quickly.
I experience the world at a comfortable sensory and emotional volume.
I feel most capable when authority is shared across team members in a domain.
I notice when a group's laughter is directed at someone rather than shared with them.
I believe that what people say matters more than how they say it.
I experience new places, people, and situations with a heightened sense of emotional arousal.
I see myself as someone who makes things happen.
I value precision in my work and take time to get the details right.
I believe that the moral weight of suffering is the ultimate measure of whether a civilization is succeeding or failing at its most fundamental task.
I find that the moral demand of suffering is the experience that has given me the deepest understanding of what it means to be part of a moral community.
I see group work as an opportunity to learn from other people's priorities.
I find that engaging with human problems through a framework of logic and structure produces more equitable results than engaging through feeling.
I let the boundaries between different areas of my life blur naturally.
I consider it a compliment when people describe me as easygoing.
A tense silence in a conversation produces a physical knot in my stomach almost instantly.
I tend to structure group decisions around outcomes that benefit me most.
I feel that groups need a strong anchor, and I provide that.
I feel that an adaptable person is a valuable person.
I often initiate change rather than wait for conditions to force it.
I detect when someone deliberately mispronounces a word or name to signal familiarity or distance.
